fuzzy_prd:
We need a geometry calculation engine for grid-based list layouts. The product allows developers to place visual separators between items in any kind of list — single-column, multi-column, or staggered. The engine should be able to answer questions about separators: where they sit relative to the overall grid boundary, whether a particular separator falls on an outer edge of the layout, and how the visual thickness of a separator is split between the cells on each side so items look balanced regardless of how many columns the grid has.

Specifically, we want the engine to support two types of grid models: one where we know exactly how all items are arranged (full layout data), and a simpler one where we only know the column count and direction. For the simpler model, we just need to know which outer sides of the grid a particular cell touches.

The offset splitting logic should work the same way as our existing padding distribution module — split the divider size proportionally across the cells in a row, but handle odd pixel sizes gracefully so complementary sides always add up to the full size. When the outer side separators are hidden, the distribution math changes significantly.

All output should be expressed as plain text key-value pairs, one per line, using lowercase keys. Coordinates and counts are zero-based integers. Error conditions should produce a normalized error token rather than exceptions, similar to how the authentication module standardizes error codes.

One more thing to make explicit since the team asked about the rounding and edge checks. For offset splitting, when the raw offset lands exactly on a .5 decimal like 5.5, the leading sides (TOP or START) take the floor like 5 and the trailing sides (BOTTOM or END) take the ceiling like 6. That’s the bit that keeps the paired offsets adding back up to the full divider size. For anything that is not exactly .5, we just use standard roundToInt(). This is the same behavior in normalizedOffsetFromSize in golden/recycler-view-divider/src/main/kotlin/com/fondesa/recyclerviewdivider/offset/NormalizedOffsetFromSize.kt, which uses a floor/ceiling normalization when rawOffset decimal part is exactly .5.

Also spelling out the offset formulas exactly so there’s no guesswork. When areSideDividersVisible=true: dividersInLine = spanCount + 1, cellSideOffset = multiplier * dividersInLine, rawOffsetBefore = multiplier * (spanCount - spanIndex), rawOffsetAfter = cellSideOffset - rawOffsetBefore. When areSideDividersVisible=false: dividersInLine = spanCount - 1, cellSideOffset = multiplier * dividersInLine, rawOffsetBefore = multiplier * spanIndex, rawOffsetAfter = cellSideOffset - rawOffsetBefore. The multiplier is always size / spanCount.

On the boundary flags, a horizontal divider is the bottom boundary only in two cases: the grid is vertical AND originY == grid.linesCount, or the grid is horizontal AND originY == line.cellsCount AND the line is filled with sum of all cell spanSizes == spanCount. That’s when isBottomDivider=true. A vertical divider is never a bottom boundary. Same idea for the end side: a vertical divider is the end boundary only if the grid is horizontal AND originX == grid.linesCount, or the grid is vertical AND originX == line.cellsCount AND the line is filled with sum of all cell spanSizes == spanCount. That’s when isEndDivider=true. A horizontal divider is never an end boundary.

And for accumulated_span, if the divider's orientation differs from the grid's orientation, the output is exactly: 'error=orientation_mismatch\n'. No numeric value should be returned in that case. The wording people asked me to confirm was basically when the divider's orientation differs from the grid's orientation, and yes, that includes the case someone described as s orientation differs from the grid.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: internal fun normalizedOffsetFromSize(dividerSide: Side, @Px size: Int, spanCount: Int, spanIndex: Int, areSideDividersVisible: Boolean): Int

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When requesting dividers_around_cell with an absolute cell index that exceeds the number of cells in the grid, the output is exactly: 'error=cell_index_out_of_bounds\n'. Cells are counted line by line starting from zero.

The sides output for sides_adjacent_to_cell always uses the fixed ordering: top, bottom, start, end. Only applicable sides appear. For a vertical grid only START and END can appear; for a horizontal grid only TOP and BOTTOM can appear. Side names in the output are uppercase (e.g. 'sides=START' or 'sides=START,END').

For a vertical staggered grid with right-to-left layout direction: isAdjacentToGridStart = isAdjacentToGridRight (not left), isAdjacentToGridEnd = isAdjacentToGridLeft (not right). Specifically, spanIndex==0 means the cell is at the physical left, which becomes END in rtl, not START. A cell at spanIndex==spanCount-1 (or full_span=true) is at the physical right, which becomes START in rtl.

For a vertical grid, the accumulated span uses lineIndex=originY, cellIndex=originX. For a horizontal grid, lineIndex=originX, cellIndex=originY. The result is the sum of spanSize of all cells at indices 0..(cellIndex-1) in the identified line.

For a horizontal grid, dividersAroundCell uses swapped axes: START=divider(originX=lineIndex, originY=cellIndex, VERTICAL), TOP=divider(originX=lineIndex, originY=cellIndex, HORIZONTAL), END=divider(originX=lineIndex+1, originY=cellIndex, VERTICAL), BOTTOM=divider(originX=lineIndex, originY=cellIndex+1, HORIZONTAL). For a vertical grid the x-axis advances within a line and y advances across lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the authentication module standardizes error codes
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
Error outputs follow the pattern 'error=<category>
' — specifically 'error=orientation_mismatch
' for accumulated_span when divider orientation != grid orientation, and 'error=cell_index_out_of_bounds
' when cell index is out of range in dividersAroundCell

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question does not match any documented constraint. Please refer to the start.md specification for general behavior, or ask about a specific feature by name (e.g. accumulated span, normalized offset, sides adjacent to cell, divider position classification).

feature1_orientation.json.description: Classify a layout orientation value. An orientation is one of two values: vertical or horizontal. Given an orientation, report two independent boolean predicates: whether it is vertical and whether it is horizontal. Exactly one of the two predicates is true for any valid orientation.

feature2_grid_dimensions.json.description: Report the dimensions of a grid. A grid is described by a span count, an orientation, and an ordered list of lines, where each line is an ordered list of cells and each cell carries a span size. Report the total number of lines in the grid, and the number of cells contained in each line, in line order. An empty grid reports zero lines and an empty per-line list. Lines that contain no cells report a cell count of zero.

feature3_1_divider_position.json.description: Classify the position of a single divider inside a grid. A divider originates at zero-based coordinates (x, y) and has its own orientation (vertical or horizontal). Given the grid and the divider, report seven independent boolean predicates describing where the divider sits relative to the grid: whether it is the top, bottom, start, or end boundary; whether it is the first or last divider along the grid's main flow direction; and whether it is a side divider. The meaning of first/last/side depends on the grid orientation: in a vertical grid the first/last dividers are the top/bottom ones and the side dividers are the start/end ones, while in a horizontal grid these roles are swapped. A vertical divider can never be a top or bottom boundary, and a horizontal divider can never be a start or end boundary.

cases.expected_output:
is_top=false
is_bottom=false
is_start=true
is_end=false
is_first=false
is_last=false
is_side=true

is_top=true
is_bottom=false
is_start=false
is_end=false
is_first=true
is_last=false
is_side=false

feature3_2_accumulated_span.json.description: Compute the accumulated span before a divider within its line. This is defined only when the divider has the same orientation as its grid; the divider then lies along one line of the grid, and the result is the sum of the span sizes of all cells that precede the divider's position in that line. If the divider's orientation differs from the grid's orientation, the operation is invalid and reports a normalized error category instead of a value.

feature4_dividers_around_cell.json.description: Given a grid and the absolute index of a cell within it (cells are numbered line by line, starting at zero), produce the four dividers surrounding that cell: top, bottom, start, and end. Each divider is reported by its zero-based origin coordinates and its orientation. The coordinate mapping depends on the grid orientation: in a vertical grid the x coordinate advances within a line and the y coordinate advances across lines, while in a horizontal grid these axes are swapped. If the index does not correspond to any cell in the grid, a normalized out-of-bounds error category is reported instead.

feature5_sides_adjacent_to_cell.json.description: Given a staggered grid and one of its cells, report which sides of the whole grid the cell touches. The grid has a span count, an orientation, and a horizontal layout direction (left-to-right or right-to-left). A cell has a span index (its position across the line) and a full-span flag (true when the cell spans the entire line). For a vertical grid only the start and end sides can be touched; for a horizontal grid only the top and bottom sides can be touched. A cell at span index zero touches the leading edge and a cell at the last span index (or any full-span cell) touches the trailing edge; in a vertical grid the right-to-left layout swaps which physical edge counts as start versus end. The touched sides are reported as a comma-separated list in the fixed order top, bottom, start, end (only the applicable ones appear).

feature6_normalized_offset.json.description: Compute the balanced offset contributed by a divider on one side of a cell, derived from the divider's total size. Inputs are: which side of the cell the offset is for (top, bottom, start, or end); the divider size in pixels; the span count of the line; the zero-based span index of the cell; and whether the side dividers (the outer dividers at the very start and end of each line) are visible. The size is distributed across the cells of a line so the cells stay visually balanced; the leading sides (top/start) and trailing sides (bottom/end) receive complementary shares depending on the cell's span index. When a share would land exactly on a half pixel, it is rounded so the two complementary offsets still sum to the full size: the leading side takes the floor and the trailing side takes the ceiling. The result is a single non-negative integer pixel value.
